learning.summary: Task Manager is a Windows utility used to view and manage running apps, background processes, performance, startup apps, users, details, and services.
learning.explanation: Use Task Manager when you need a quick view of what is running on a Windows system or need to stop an unresponsive app. It is a live activity tool, not a historical log viewer.

facts.text: The Processes tab shows running apps and background processes.
The Performance tab shows live CPU, memory, disk, network, and GPU activity.
The Startup tab can enable or disable startup apps.
The Services tab can view services and open the full Services console.

examples.text: Use Task Manager to end a frozen app.
Use the Startup tab to reduce boot-time startup programs.

examTips.text: For a frozen application, Task Manager is usually the first tool to end the task.

commonMistakes.text: Confusing Task Manager with Event Viewer. Task Manager shows current activity; Event Viewer shows logs.
Assuming disabling startup apps uninstalls them. It only prevents automatic startup.

scenarios.situation: A user says an application is frozen and will not close.
scenarios.expectedAction: Open Task Manager and end the affected process or application.

pbqIdeas.task: Match each Task Manager tab to its use: Processes, Performance, Startup, Users, Details, Services.

questionTargets.promptFocus: Match Task Manager tabs to their purposes.

related.reason: Task Manager can display services and open the full Services console.
Both are used to evaluate system performance, but Performance Monitor is more detailed.
Often compared with Task Manager during troubleshooting.

contrastsWith.reason: Task Manager shows live activity; Event Viewer shows historical logs.
